Trek through the verdant mountains surrounding Leishan to discover terraced rice fields, bamboo groves, and panoramic views of the karst landscape.
Participate in hands-on sessions learning traditional indigo dyeing and batik techniques that have characterized Miao textiles for generations.

food.detail: A signature Miao dish combining fresh river fish with sour bamboo shoots, wild herbs, and chilies, creating a complex flavor balance essential to local cuisine.
Sticky rice wrapped in fresh bamboo leaves and often filled with Chinese dates or beans, a staple food for Miao celebrations and daily consumption.
A savory beverage made from roasted tea leaves, oil, and broth, often served with puffed rice crackers and preserved vegetables as a welcoming drink.
Traditionally smoked Miao pork preserved over open fires, delivering deep smoky flavors and often served sliced with chili oil or in rice dishes.
A seasonal preparation featuring foraged mountain mushrooms cooked in a spiced broth with local vegetables and proteins, reflecting Miao use of forest ingredients.

culture.detail: Miao women wear significant amounts of handcrafted silver jewelry as both cultural identity and wealth display; showing genuine interest in their pieces is appreciated.
November's Guzang Festival is the peak season when villages are most vibrant; book accommodations well in advance and expect increased visitor presence during this period.
While Mandarin is understood, hiring a local Miao-speaking guide significantly enriches cultural interactions and provides access to family homes and private ceremonies.
Always ask permission before photographing villagers or ceremonies, as some Miao traditions hold spiritual significance and not all residents welcome cameras.

places.name: Longgong Cavern
places.description: An extensive underground karst cave system with underground rivers, crystal-clear pools, and illuminated limestone formations spanning multiple chambers. The cavern is one of China's most impressive subterranean landscapes with boat rides through underground waterways.

activities.detail: Navigate through illuminated underground rivers on electric boats passing through multiple cavern chambers with towering limestone formations.
Observe the transparent underground pools reflecting the cavern ceiling and stalactite formations in the Dragon Palace chamber.
Photograph dramatic stalactites, stalagmites, and flowstone cascades illuminated by the cave's colored lighting systems throughout the main passages.
Walk through designated pathways to witness underground waterfalls and rapids within the lower levels of the cavern system.
Visit the largest chamber featuring soaring ceilings, colossal stone pillars, and intricate limestone carvings named after the mythical dragon king.
Cross narrow bridges spanning underground rivers and chasms for closer views of the cavern's deepest sections and geological features.

food.detail: A signature regional dish using local river fish in a sour broth made from sour plums and chilies, commonly served in restaurants near the cavern entrance.
Traditional glutinous rice cakes filled with sweet or savory fillings, a popular local snack sold at cavern gift shops and nearby food stalls.
A creamy, aromatic noodle soup made with fried rice flour, sesame, and local herbs, warming and traditional to the Guizhou mountain region.
Rice cooked inside bamboo segments with local mushrooms and mountain herbs, authentic to minority communities in the Anshun area surrounding Longgong.

culture.detail: The cavern pathways are frequently wet and slippery due to moisture and mist; sturdy, non-slip footwear is essential for safety on walkways and bridges.
Longgong is named after the Chinese dragon king mythology; visitors should be respectful when photographing the Dragon Palace main hall as it holds spiritual significance for local communities.
Underground temperatures remain cool year-round (approximately 10-15°C); a light jacket or sweater is necessary despite warm outdoor weather in Guizhou.
Guided boat tours have limited capacity and fill quickly, especially during peak summer and holiday seasons; advance booking through local tourism offices is recommended.

places.name: Qingyan Ancient Town
places.description: A well-preserved Ming and Qing dynasty town featuring stone-paved streets, traditional architecture, and historic temples just outside Guiyang. It's easily accessible from the capital and offers a charming taste of old China with excellent local snacks and crafts.

activities.detail: Walk through the atmospheric Ming and Qing dynasty alleyways lined with stone blocks, traditional wooden shutters, and lantern-hung archways that transport you centuries back in time.
Tour this 600-year-old Buddhist temple complex featuring intricate carvings, serene courtyards, and displays of religious artifacts from the Ming dynasty.
Ascend the well-preserved fortification walls that once protected the town to enjoy panoramic views of the surrounding countryside and architectural layout.
Visit artisan studios where skilled craftspeople demonstrate traditional techniques for stone carving, wood carving, embroidery, and pottery production.
Capture images of the ornate entrance arches and stone gates that mark the boundaries of the ancient town, each decorated with classical calligraphy and carved motifs.
Experience traditional Peking opera and folk performances in an authentic restored opera house that showcases cultural entertainment as locals enjoyed it centuries ago.

food.detail: These signature steamed pork and glutinous rice dumplings are savory, tender, and traditionally wrapped in a leaf casing, representing the town's most iconic snack.
Sweet glutinous rice cakes pounded into smooth rounds and stuffed with sesame, peanut, or brown sugar fillings, sold fresh at street stalls throughout the town.
Silky handmade tofu prepared using traditional stone-grinding methods and served in various preparations including soup, pan-fried, or cold salad form.
A tangy Guizhou specialty featuring fresh local fish cooked in a broth flavored with pickled vegetables, chilies, and local spices unique to the region.
River snails stir-fried with aromatic chili peppers, Sichuan peppercorns, and fragrant spices, challenging but rewarding to eat from the shell.

culture.detail: The ancient stone streets and uneven pathways require sturdy footwear to safely navigate the town and minimize risk of slipping or injury.

culture.text: Visit Early Morning or Late Afternoon
culture.detail: Arrive before 10 AM or after 4 PM to avoid large tour groups and experience the town's authentic atmosphere when locals are more active in daily routines.
When entering religious sites, dress modestly, remove shoes if instructed, and avoid loud conversations or photography during active worship periods out of respect.
Light negotiation is expected when purchasing crafts and souvenirs from vendors, but maintain a friendly demeanor and accept offers gracefully without aggressive haggling.

places.name: Wulong Karst Park
places.description: A spectacular karst landscape featuring dramatic sinkholes, natural bridges, and deep gorges with excellent hiking trails and scenic viewpoints. The geology offers insights into China's most impressive limestone formations and dramatic natural architecture.

activities.detail: Trek across the iconic natural stone bridge spanning 300 meters with panoramic views of surrounding sinkholes and limestone peaks.
Descend into a deep limestone gorge featuring a underground river, ancient cave dwellings, and dramatic cliff walls carved by water erosion.
Explore a massive stalactite-filled cavern with illuminated chambers revealing intricate mineral formations and underground lakes.
View a spectacular waterfall cascading from the karst cliffs into a emerald pool surrounded by lush vegetation.
Stand at elevated viewpoints overlooking multiple sinkholes with vertical walls creating surreal landscape photography opportunities.
Scale limestone cliffs using secured climbing routes that offer adrenaline-pumping ascents with stunning aerial perspectives of the karst terrain.

food.detail: Fresh bamboo shoots harvested from the surrounding forests are prepared stir-fried, steamed, or in soups, offering tender texture and subtle earthy flavor.
Local freshwater fish cooked in traditional clay pots with medicinal herbs and local spices, a warming regional specialty.
Wild greens unique to the karst region gathered from limestone areas, typically blanched or stir-fried with garlic.
A sweet herbal liqueur made from cornelian cherry fruits grown in Wulong's valleys, traditionally enjoyed as a digestif or gift.
Sticky rice cooked inside hollowed bamboo segments with local fillings like mushrooms or preserved vegetables, served as a portable trail food.

culture.detail: The karst terrain features sharp limestone edges and uneven surfaces that demand proper footwear to prevent injury during exploration.

culture.text: Bring Ample Water and Sun Protection
culture.detail: High altitude viewpoints offer minimal shade and intense UV exposure, so visitors should carry 2+ liters of water and strong sunscreen.
The region is home to Miao ethnic groups living in traditional villages; visitors should ask permission before photographing locals and purchase crafts directly from artisans.

culture.text: Best Visit Season is Spring to Autumn
culture.detail: Winter months bring fog that obscures views, while the dry season from April through October provides optimal visibility and stable trail conditions.

places.name: Fanjingshan Mountain
places.description: A sacred Buddhist mountain UNESCO site rising 2493 meters with distinctive twin peaks, ancient temples, and mist-shrouded trails offering panoramic views. The mountain is venerated as a pilgrimage destination and features biodiversity hotspot with unique flora and fauna.

activities.detail: Hike to the two distinctive twin peaks at 2493 meters where the Golden Hall temple sits perched between them, offering 360-degree panoramic views of mist-covered valleys.
Visit the ancient Buddhist temple complex nestled on the mountainside, featuring traditional architecture and serving as a major pilgrimage destination for devotees.
Experience the ethereal phenomenon of sea clouds rolling through the valleys at sunrise or sunset, best viewed from high elevation vantage points along the trails.
Trek through pristine forest zones to spot endemic species including the Guizhou snub-nosed monkey, golden pheasants, and rare alpine flora found only on this UNESCO site.
Visit the iconic mushroom-shaped rock formations scattered across the mountain ridges, popular spots for photography and meditation.
Sit in contemplation at the historic Old Golden Hall ruins, an ancient meditation site with spiritual significance in Chinese Buddhism.
Enjoy exceptional stargazing from the high elevation peaks where light pollution is minimal and visibility of the Milky Way is outstanding.

food.detail: A savory traditional beverage made from roasted tea leaves, sesame, and peanuts mixed with hot water, commonly served at mountain guesthouses for warmth and energy.
Fresh spring bamboo shoots harvested from surrounding forests, prepared steamed or stir-fried with local seasonings and served in mountain restaurants.
Hearty soup featuring wild mushrooms foraged from the biodiversity-rich slopes, simmered with local herbs and mountain vegetables.
A local specialty of purple-hued sticky rice steamed with chestnuts and dates, offering sustained energy for hikers and pilgrims.
Locally caught freshwater fish preserved in a traditional sour brine made from fermented grains, representing regional flavor profiles.
Free-range mountain chicken stewed slowly with traditional Chinese medicinal herbs believed to restore vitality after strenuous hiking.

culture.detail: Visitors should remove shoes before entering temple halls, avoid pointing at religious statues, and observe quiet reverence during prayer times when pilgrims are present.
Locals and pilgrims begin ascents before dawn to reach summits by sunrise and avoid afternoon mists; joining this rhythm ensures best views and safety.
The mountain reaches 2493 meters; taking time to acclimate and carrying water respectfully reflects understanding of local mountaineering wisdom.
During major Buddhist festivals and pilgrimage seasons, expect large gatherings of devotees; maintain respectful distance and avoid disrupting spiritual ceremonies or processions.

restaurants.name: Lao Kaoya
restaurants.description: Celebrated local institution famous for perfectly roasted duck and traditional Guizhou flavors that define the province's culinary identity.

restaurants.name: Qian Wei Lou
restaurants.description: Multi-level restaurant blending spicy Hunan cooking with Guizhou specialties, packed with local diners seeking bold, authentic flavors.

restaurants.name: Chen Ji Mala Tang
restaurants.description: No-frills hotpot spot where diners select fresh ingredients to cook in numbing Sichuan broths, offering excellent value and authentic heat.
restaurants.mustOrder: Mala Broth with Beef Offal (麻辣牛杂)

restaurants.name: Tian Xia
restaurants.description: Rustic farmhouse restaurant celebrating wild mushrooms, preserved vegetables, and highland meats prepared with traditional mountain techniques.

restaurants.name: Huang Ji Lao Dian
restaurants.description: Family-run establishment serving authentic Miao minority cuisine with sour soups, preserved fish, and hand-pounded glutinous rice specialties.

restaurants.name: Shang Pin
restaurants.description: Upscale dining showcasing Miao and Dong ethnic minority recipes with refined plating and rare local ingredients sourced from surrounding villages.
restaurants.mustOrder: Dong Sticky Rice with Herb Chicken (侗家糯米饭)

restaurants.name: Liu Yi Si
restaurants.description: Modest noodle shop famous for hand-pulled noodles and local rice noodles served in aromatic broths, beloved by morning commuters.
restaurants.mustOrder: Hand-Pulled Noodles in Pork Broth (手工面)

restaurants.name: Qian Lou
restaurants.description: Multi-story establishment offering Anshun specialties including acid-sour snail dishes and spicy preserved vegetables in warm, welcoming surroundings.

restaurants.name: Wei Lai Gong
restaurants.description: Unpretentious family restaurant serving hearty miners' fare—grilled meats, stir-fried seasonal vegetables, and thick noodle soups.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled Pork Ribs with Soy Sauce (酱油烤排骨)

restaurants.name: Long Xia He
restaurants.description: Casual crayfish specialist celebrating both Dong minority traditions and freshwater crustaceans from Tongren's rivers, spiced to preference.

restaurants.name: Xiang Shu
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant highlighting Bijie's high-altitude produce—local beans, grains, and pasture-raised meats prepared with modern technique.
restaurants.mustOrder: Roasted Highland Chicken with Wild Herbs (高原烤鸡)

restaurants.name: Tian Xia Yi Hao
restaurants.description: Elegant fine-dining establishment elevating provincial recipes with seasonal tasting menus and rare aged spirits, showcasing Guizhou's culinary heritage.
restaurants.mustOrder: Multi-Course Tasting Menu of Guizhou Classics (贵州八宝)

restaurants.name: Niu Jia Ban
restaurants.description: Iconic noodle house perfecting the famous Liuzhou luosifen—rice noodles in pungent snail broth that draws devotees from across China.

restaurants.name: Gui Zhou Hao Wei
restaurants.description: Family kitchen specializing in Buyi and Zhuang minority foods—pounded glutinous rice, herb-wrapped grilled fish, and fermented bean pastes.

regions.name: Beijing, Tianjin & Hebei
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring (April-May) or autumn (September-October) to avoid harsh winters and summer heat.

regions.name: Northeast (Manchuria)
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in summer (June-August) for comfortable weather, or winter (December-February) for snow festivals.

regions.name: Inner Mongolia

top10.name: Beijing
top10.description: China's capital and political heart, blending ancient imperial heritage with cutting-edge modernity across 16 districts. Essential for experiencing traditional culture, world-class museums, hutong neighborhoods, and contemporary Asian dynamism.

top10.name: Shanghai
top10.description: China's largest city and a global financial hub where ultramodern skyscrapers meet colonial architecture along the Huangpu River. The Bund, Yu Garden, and Oriental Pearl Tower showcase the city's blend of tradition and cutting-edge modernity.

top10.name: Great Wall of China
top10.description: The world's longest fortification spanning over 13,000 miles, with the most accessible and well-preserved sections near Beijing at Badaling and Mutianyu. A symbol of China and one of humanity's most impressive architectural feats.

top10.name: Guilin
top10.description: Guilin is renowned for its dramatic karst mountains rising from the Li River, offering some of China's most iconic natural scenery. The city serves as the gateway to river cruises and hiking adventures through stunning limestone peaks dotted with caves and traditional villages.

top10.name: Xi'an
top10.description: China's ancient capital and one of the world's most historically important cities, Xi'an is home to the famous Terracotta Army and sits along the Wei River, a major Yellow River tributary. The city's ancient walls, museums, and Buddhist temples make it essential for understanding Chinese civilization.

top10.name: Hangzhou
top10.description: Famous for its stunning West Lake with misty mountains and classical pavilions, Hangzhou is a spiritual and romantic destination that has inspired artists for centuries. The lake-side scenery, ancient temples, and excellent tea culture create an unforgettable experience.

top10.name: Forbidden City
top10.description: The largest palace complex in the world and former home of Chinese emperors, spanning nearly 1,000 buildings across 72 hectares. A UNESCO World Heritage site essential for understanding imperial Chinese history and architecture.

top10.name: Zhangjiajie
top10.description: Famous for its towering sandstone pillars that inspired Avatar's landscape, Zhangjiajie National Forest Park is one of China's most spectacular natural wonders. Visitors can hike through mist-shrouded mountains, cross glass bridges, and witness breathtaking panoramic views.

top10.name: Chengdu
top10.description: The capital of Sichuan Province, famous as the giant panda capital of the world and a center of Chinese culture and cuisine. Home to the Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding and vibrant teahouses, street food, and ancient temples.

top10.name: Nanjing
top10.description: A historic capital with profound cultural significance, Nanjing offers imperial relics, the magnificent Ming Xiaoling Mausoleum, and the serene Confucius Temple along the Qinhuai River. The city combines ancient Chinese heritage with modern vibrancy and excellent museums.

nanning.credit: K X I T H V I S U A L S

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 42 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Spring Festival (Chinese New Year)
festivals.description: China's most important festival celebrating the lunar new year with family reunions, fireworks, dragon dances, and traditional feasts lasting 15 days. It's a time of cultural renewal and the busiest travel period in China.

festivals.name: Harbin Ice and Snow Festival
festivals.description: The world's largest ice sculpture festival featuring massive illuminated ice palaces, sculptures, and winter activities in the frozen Songhua River. It transforms the city into a magical winter wonderland.

festivals.name: Lantern Festival
festivals.description: Celebrated on the 15th day of the lunar new year, featuring lantern displays, dragon dances, and traditional sweet dumplings. It marks the end of Spring Festival celebrations.

festivals.name: Qingming Festival
festivals.description: A national holiday for tomb sweeping and honoring ancestors with family gatherings and outdoor activities marking the start of spring. Families visit gravesites with offerings and pay respects.

festivals.name: Pear Flower Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of blooming pear blossoms in Lüliang with cultural performances and agricultural displays showcasing local harvests. The white flowers create stunning landscapes across the valleys.

festivals.name: Dragon Boat Festival
festivals.description: A traditional festival featuring dragon boat races, zongzi (glutinous rice dumplings), and cultural performances commemorating poet Qu Yuan. Communities across China hold colorful racing competitions on waterways.

festivals.name: Midi Music Festival
festivals.description: One of Asia's largest rock and pop music festivals attracting international and Chinese artists with multiple stages and performances. It's a major event for music lovers seeking contemporary Asian culture.

festivals.name: Longsheng Rice Terrace Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the stunning Longsheng Rice Terraces with minority cultural performances, agricultural traditions, and harvest festivities. Visitors experience the Zhuang and Yao peoples' vibrant traditions.

festivals.name: Zhongshan Lantern Festival
festivals.description: A colorful celebration featuring elaborate lantern displays, traditional Cantonese performances, and local food festivals. The festival celebrates the region's rich cultural heritage.

festivals.name: Naadam Festival
festivals.description: The 'Olympics of the steppes' featuring traditional Mongol sports including horse racing, wrestling, and archery with colorful festivities and nomadic culture displays. It's the largest cultural event in Inner Mongolia.

festivals.name: Mid-Autumn Festival
festivals.description: A major lunar calendar festival celebrating family reunion with mooncakes, lanterns, and traditional performances under the full moon. Families gather to give thanks and enjoy festive meals.

festivals.name: Chrysanthemum Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of blooming chrysanthemums in traditional gardens with flower exhibitions, cultural performances, and art displays. Kaifeng is famous for hosting one of China's oldest flower festivals.

festivals.name: National Day Golden Week
festivals.description: China's most important national holiday celebrating the founding of the People's Republic with parades, festivities, and mass tourism across all regions. Major attractions offer special celebrations and performances.

festivals.name: Shoton Festival (Yogurt Festival)
festivals.description: A traditional Tibetan Buddhist festival featuring opera performances, giant thangka displays, and yogurt consumption celebrating the end of summer monasteries' retreats. It's a vibrant blend of religious and cultural traditions.

festivals.name: Potala Palace Pilgrimage Festival
festivals.description: A spiritual gathering where pilgrims visit the sacred Potala Palace in Lhasa for religious devotions and traditional ceremonies honoring Tibetan Buddhism. The white-washed walls are beautifully maintained during this period.

festivals.name: Kashgar Sunday Bazaar
festivals.description: A vibrant weekly market showcasing Uyghur culture, traditional crafts, spices, textiles, and livestock trading with authentic Central Asian atmosphere. It's one of the world's most colorful traditional markets.

festivals.name: Urumqi International Grand Bazaar Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Silk Road culture featuring traditional performances, crafts markets, and multicultural cuisine representing Xinjiang's diverse ethnic groups. It attracts visitors from across Central Asia.

festivals.name: Mount Tai International Tourism Festival
festivals.description: A festival celebrating one of China's sacred mountains with hiking events, cultural performances, and traditional rituals. Mount Tai holds significant spiritual importance in Chinese culture.

festivals.name: Yuanyang Rice Terrace Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the UNESCO-listed Yuanyang Rice Terraces with Hani minority culture, traditional practices, and stunning landscape photography opportunities during golden season. The terraces reflect sunlight creating magical patterns.

festivals.name: Lijiang Old Town Festival
festivals.description: A cultural event in the ancient Naxi settlement featuring traditional music, dance, and ceremonies preserving the ethnic heritage of southwestern China. The cobblestone streets host performances and cultural exhibitions.

festivals.name: Torch Festival
festivals.description: An ancient Yi, Bai, and Naxi ethnic festival featuring torch processions, wrestling competitions, and bonfires celebrating prosperity and warding off evil spirits. It's a spectacular display of fire and cultural traditions.

festivals.name: Shuangxi Lantern Festival
festivals.description: A traditional festival featuring elaborate lantern displays, folk performances, and temple fairs in ancient Shuangxi village. The illuminated lanterns create magical nighttime scenes.

festivals.name: Kite Flying Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of traditional Chinese kite culture with international competitions and displays in the ancient city of Weifang. The skies fill with colorful handmade kites of various designs.

festivals.name: West Lake International Expo
festivals.description: A major cultural and business exposition at the scenic West Lake featuring art exhibitions, international performances, and traditional Chinese culture showcases. The beautiful lakeside setting enhances the experience.

festivals.name: Qingyuan Longmu Lake Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Guangdong's natural beauty with water sports, cultural performances, and local cuisine festivals at the scenic Longmu Lake. Traditional Cantonese activities highlight regional heritage.

festivals.name: Fujian Tea Culture Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Fujian's famous oolong and black tea traditions with tea tastings, cultural exhibitions, and traditional ceremonies. The region is renowned for producing some of China's finest teas.

festivals.name: Pingtan Blue Tears Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the rare bioluminescent plankton phenomenon creating glowing blue waters at Pingtan Beach. Visitors experience this magical natural light show during calm nights.

festivals.name: Shaanxi History Culture Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of ancient Chinese civilization featuring archaeological exhibitions, traditional performances, and historical reenactments. Xi'an's rich imperial heritage is showcased through various cultural events.

festivals.name: Terracotta Warriors Lighting Festival
festivals.description: Evening illuminations and cultural performances at the famous Terracotta Warriors site creating a spectacular nighttime experience of ancient China's most iconic discovery. The lighting design showcases the warriors beautifully.

festivals.name: Zhangye Danxia Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the colorful geological formations with photography contests, hiking events, and cultural performances showcasing northwestern China's dramatic landscapes. The rainbow mountains attract artists and photographers worldwide.

festivals.name: Mogao Caves Cultural Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the ancient Buddhist art and Silk Road heritage with exhibitions, lectures, and cultural performances at this UNESCO World Heritage site. The caves contain thousands of years of Buddhist artistic traditions.

festivals.name: Qinghai Lake Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the world's highest salt lake featuring sports competitions, cultural performances, and environmental awareness activities with Tibetan and Mongolian traditions. The scenic lake setting is spectacular during summer.

festivals.name: Kumbum Monastery Festival
festivals.description: An important Tibetan Buddhist festival featuring butter lamp displays, religious ceremonies, and spiritual gatherings at one of Tibetan Buddhism's most sacred monasteries. Thousands of pilgrims participate in the celebrations.

festivals.name: Stone Forest Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Yunnan's unique landscape featuring Yi ethnic performances, torch festivals, and hiking events in this UNESCO World Heritage geological site. The dramatic karst formations create an otherworldly setting.

festivals.name: Guilin Lantern Festival on Li River
festivals.description: A stunning display of traditional lanterns floating on the Li River with cormorant fishing demonstrations and classical music performances. The lantern-lit landscape reflects China's poetic traditions.

festivals.name: Yangshuo Climbing Festival
festivals.description: An international rock climbing competition and festival in the scenic karst landscape of Yangshuo attracting climbers worldwide. The dramatic limestone peaks provide world-class climbing challenges.

festivals.name: Luoping Rape Flower Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of blooming yellow rape flowers covering vast hillsides creating stunning natural carpets with cultural performances and photography opportunities. One of China's most spectacular springtime landscapes.

festivals.name: Huanglong Temple Fair
festivals.description: A traditional temple fair at the sacred Huanglong site featuring religious ceremonies, folk performances, and cultural celebrations blending Buddhism with local traditions. The travertine terraces provide a sacred natural backdrop.

festivals.name: Jiuzhaigou Golden Autumn Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of autumn colors in the stunning Jiuzhaigou Valley with hiking events, photography contests, and cultural performances as the landscape transforms to gold and red. The scenic valley is at its most photogenic during this season.

festivals.name: Zhaoqing Seven-Star Crags Festival
festivals.description: A cultural celebration featuring boating, traditional performances, and scenic tourism at the ancient limestone peaks forming the shape of the Big Dipper constellation. The karst landscape offers stunning natural beauty.

festivals.name: Anhui Poetry and Tea Culture Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of classical Chinese poetry and Anhui's famous Huangshan tea with cultural salons, poetry readings, and tea tastings in historic mountain settings. The festival honors literati traditions of ancient China.

festivals.name: Suzhou Garden Lantern Festival
festivals.description: An enchanting display of traditional lanterns illuminating the classical gardens of Suzhou with performances and cultural exhibitions celebrating the region's artistic heritage. The UNESCO gardens are transformed by dramatic lighting.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of China
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 15 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Paleolithic and Neolithic China
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers gradually adopted agriculture along the Yellow and Yangtze rivers.
items.text: Early humans inhabited China for hundreds of thousands of years, but the real transformation came after the last ice age when communities began cultivating millet in the north and rice in the south. These agricultural settlements grew more sophisticated, developing pottery, tools, and social hierarchies. By 3000 BC, distinct regional cultures like the Yangshao and Longshan had emerged, laying foundations for organized states. The discovery of oracle bones from this period shows early attempts at writing and divination, hinting at religious and administrative complexity.

items.label: Xia Dynasty
items.summary: China's legendary first dynasty established hereditary rule and urban civilization.
items.text: The Xia dynasty marks the traditional beginning of Chinese civilization, though archaeological evidence remains debated by scholars. According to legends passed down through centuries, the Xia ruled the Yellow River valley with a system of hereditary succession that replaced earlier tribal leadership. They allegedly developed early bronze technology, though surviving artifacts are scarce and often controversial. This era represents the transition from prehistory to recorded history in Chinese tradition, establishing patterns of dynastic rule that would define China for millennia.

items.label: Shang Dynasty
items.summary: Bronze-age warriors ruled through oracle bones and ancestral worship.
items.text: The Shang dynasty is the first Chinese dynasty confirmed by archaeological evidence, with spectacular bronze vessels and oracle bone records proving its sophistication. Shang kings ruled from cities like Zhengzhou and Anyang, commanding armies of chariots and bronze-armed warriors who fought neighboring states and enslaved peoples. They developed an early form of writing on oracle bones—heated animal bones and shells used for divination—which reveals their obsession with communicating with ancestors and predicting the future. Bronze technology reached remarkable heights under Shang craftsmen, who created intricate ritual vessels that demonstrated both technical mastery and artistic vision, establishing aesthetic standards that influenced Chinese art for thousands of years.

items.label: Zhou Dynasty
items.summary: China's longest dynasty fragmented into competing kingdoms, spawning philosophy and warfare.
items.text: The Zhou dynasty lasted nearly 800 years but spent most of it in decline, beginning as a unified state and ending as a collection of warring kingdoms. Early Zhou rulers justified their conquest by introducing the 'Mandate of Heaven,' claiming divine approval for their rule—a concept that would legitimize Chinese dynasties for centuries. The latter half of the Zhou period, called the Warring States era, saw constant conflict between competing states like Qin, Chu, and Zhao, which paradoxically stimulated unprecedented cultural and intellectual flourishing. This turbulent age produced Confucius and Laozi, whose philosophies would shape Chinese civilization, and also drove military innovations and administrative reforms that one state—Qin—would use to unify all of China.

items.label: Qin Dynasty
items.summary: A brutal but brilliant state conquered all rivals and forged the first unified Chinese empire.
items.text: The Qin dynasty lasted only 15 years but accomplished what seemed impossible: the complete unification of warring China under the ruthless Qin Shi Huang. This legalist autocrat standardized weights, measures, currency, and even axle widths across the empire, creating unprecedented administrative cohesion. He ordered the construction of the Great Wall by conscripting hundreds of thousands of workers, and preserved himself for the afterlife with the famous Terracotta Army—thousands of life-sized soldiers buried in his tomb. Though the Qin burned books and suppressed dissent, it established the bureaucratic model and imperial structure that subsequent dynasties would refine and follow for two thousand years.

items.label: Han Dynasty
items.summary: A pragmatic, cultured dynasty stabilized the empire and extended Chinese influence across Asia.
items.text: The Han dynasty inherited Qin's administrative framework but softened its harshness, blending legalism with Confucian values to create a more balanced and durable system. Han emperors expanded the empire dramatically, pushing into Central Asia and establishing the Silk Road trade routes that would exchange not just goods but ideas, religions, and technologies between East and West. Han courts became centers of learning where scholars compiled the first comprehensive histories, developed new technologies like papermaking and printing, and refined artistic traditions. The Chinese people still call themselves 'Han Chinese,' a testament to this dynasty's cultural dominance and the sense of civilizational identity it created.

items.label: Three Kingdoms and Northern and Southern Dynasties
items.summary: Fragmentation and chaos gave way to cultural diversity and the rise of Buddhism across China.
items.text: After the Han collapsed, China splintered into competing kingdoms, and for nearly 400 years no single power could reunify the realm. This era of division paradoxically became remarkably creative, with competing courts patronizing poetry, painting, and philosophy while Buddhism spread from India and took root in Chinese culture. Northern regions were repeatedly invaded by nomadic peoples, creating a fascinating mix of Chinese and barbarian cultures that would influence later dynasties. Despite political fragmentation, this period saw technological advances in agriculture, metalworking, and military engineering, and the gradual sinification of foreign conquerors who adopted Chinese administrative systems and merged their identities with the Han Chinese.

items.label: Sui Dynasty
items.summary: A short-lived dynasty reunified China and began grand projects that bankrupted the state.
items.text: The Sui dynasty accomplished the monumental task of reunifying a fragmented China after nearly four centuries of division, imposing centralized rule and standardized laws across formerly rival regions. Emperor Yang pushed ambition to extremes, ordering massive construction projects including the Grand Canal connecting north and south, which required enormous conscripted labor and resources. These grandiose projects and costly military campaigns into Korea exhausted the treasury and triggered peasant rebellions that toppled the dynasty within decades. Though the Sui lasted only 29 years, it stabilized China and created infrastructure that would benefit later dynasties, earning it recognition as a pivotal bridge between the fractured past and the glorious Tang future.

items.label: Tang Dynasty
items.summary: The golden age of Chinese civilization saw unparalleled artistic, cultural, and military achievement.
items.text: The Tang dynasty is widely considered the zenith of Chinese civilization, a period of such cultural efflorescence that Chinese people abroad still call their communities 'Chinatowns' (literally 'Tang Streets'). Tang emperors created a cosmopolitan court that welcomed merchants, monks, and adventurers from across Asia, leading to unprecedented cultural exchange and innovation. Poetry flourished under patrons like Emperor Xuanzong, producing masterworks by Li Bai and Du Fu that remain unsurpassed; painters, sculptors, and musicians created works of astonishing beauty. The Tang military extended Chinese power into Central Asia and maintained the Silk Road trade, though costly campaigns and internal rebellions eventually weakened the state, culminating in gradual fragmentation into the Five Dynasties period.

items.label: Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms
items.summary: Rapid succession and political fragmentation tested China's resilience.
items.text: Following the Tang's collapse, China fractured into multiple competing dynasties and kingdoms, a chaotic 53-year period when five short-lived imperial lines struggled to control the north while ten separate kingdoms dominated the south. This era saw constant warfare, constant emperor succession, and tremendous suffering for common people caught between warring powers. Yet regional kingdoms like the Southern Tang fostered remarkable cultural achievements in painting, poetry, and ceramics, proving that political chaos didn't necessarily kill creativity. The period demonstrated that China's Confucian administrative systems and cultural identity were strong enough to survive fragmentation, setting the stage for rapid reunification under a new, more centralized power.

items.label: Song Dynasty
items.summary: An era of technological innovation and economic sophistication that invented the modern world's foundations.
items.text: The Song dynasty reunified China and pioneered technologies that would define modernity: gunpowder weapons, printing, maritime navigation, and early capitalism with paper money and banking systems. Song emperors, despite military weakness against northern invaders, presided over a cultured court that elevated landscape painting, pottery, and neo-Confucian philosophy to unprecedented heights. The dynasty maintained robust trade networks across the Indian Ocean and beyond, with Chinese merchants and ships dominating commerce while Song craftspeople produced exquisite ceramics and artworks. Though Song ultimately fell to Mongol conquest, its intellectual and technological achievements profoundly influenced the Renaissance and development of the modern world, making it one of history's most consequential civilizations.

items.label: Yuan Dynasty
items.summary: Mongol conquest created the world's largest land empire before Han Chinese restored native rule.
items.text: Kublai Khan established the Yuan dynasty after conquering Song China, extending Mongol rule over the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from Korea to Hungary. Mongol rulers adopted Chinese administrative systems and court rituals, blending nomadic and settled governance, though they maintained a racial hierarchy that privileged Mongols and other foreigners over Han Chinese. The Yuan enabled remarkable cultural exchange along restored Silk Roads, seeing visitors like Marco Polo travel to the lavish court of the Khan, while Chinese innovations like printing and gunpowder spread westward. Yet Mongol rule grew increasingly corrupt and resentful, facing constant Han Chinese rebellions, and by the 14th century fragmented authority, famine, and revolt made Yuan collapse inevitable, paving the way for Han restoration.

items.label: Ming Dynasty
items.summary: Han Chinese restored imperial rule, built the Great Wall, and sent fleets to dominate the Indian Ocean.
items.text: The Ming dynasty restored Han Chinese rule and revitalized Chinese civilization, becoming synonymous with cultural brilliance through the Forbidden City, the Great Wall's most iconic sections, and stunning blue-and-white porcelain that remains coveted worldwide. Early Ming emperors like Yongle sent Admiral Zheng He's massive fleets across the Indian Ocean in voyages of exploration that dwarfed later European expeditions in scale and sophistication, establishing Chinese dominance in Asian trade. Ming scholars produced encyclopedic works, painters created masterpieces of landscape and figure art, and craftspeople perfected ceramics and silk production to levels of unmatched quality. However, later Ming rulers grew increasingly isolated and inward-looking, abandoning overseas expeditions, raising walls against 'barbarian' influences, and focusing on court intrigue, ultimately weakening the state's defenses against Jurchen invasions from Manchuria.

items.label: Qing Dynasty
items.summary: Manchurian conquest created China's largest territorial extent before modernization's destabilizing impact.
items.text: The Qing dynasty began when Jurchen invaders from Manchuria conquered Ming China and established the last imperial dynasty, systematically adopting Chinese culture while maintaining military supremacy through their Manchu ethnic identity. Under the Kangxi and Qianlong emperors, the Qing expanded to its greatest territorial extent, incorporating Tibet, Xinjiang, and Mongolia while maintaining the tribute system that positioned China at the center of the Asian world order. Qing artisans and scholars perpetuated Ming cultural traditions in painting, poetry, and ceramics, though the court grew increasingly conservative and resistant to outside influence. The dynasty's decline accelerated in the 19th century as Western powers forced open China's markets through the Opium Wars, unequal treaties, and military defeats, while internal rebellion (the Taiping Rebellion) devastated vast regions, exposing the empire's vulnerability to modernity and ultimately leading to its collapse.

items.label: Republican Era and Japanese Invasion
items.summary: China transitioned from empire to republic amid warlordism, civil war, and Japanese invasion.
items.text: The Qing dynasty's fall in 1912 ended 2,000 years of imperial rule, but the Republic of China that followed fragmented into warlord-controlled regions with no central authority, leading to decades of chaos and bloodshed. This period saw the rise of the Chinese Communist Party, founded in 1921, which competed with the Nationalist government under Chiang Kai-shek for control of China while the brutal Japanese invasion from 1937 united the country temporarily against a common enemy. The Chinese Civil War resumed after Japan's defeat in 1945, with the Communists gaining momentum through rural mobilization and promises of land reform while the Nationalists faced corruption and military exhaustion. Mao Zedong's Communist forces defeated the Nationalists and proclaimed the People's Republic of China in 1949, establishing a revolutionary government that would reshape Chinese society and assert China's independence from foreign domination.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in China — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Street food stalls and wet markets
items.text: The best meals happen at unmarked stalls in alleys and night markets—look for queues of locals, not tourists. These vendors specialise in one or two dishes perfected over decades and cost 10-30 RMB per serving.

items.label: Restaurant chains vs. independents
items.text: Chain restaurants like Dicos and Yoshinoya are reliable but forgettable. Seek out family-run neighbourhood spots with handwritten menus and DIY décor—these have authentic flavour and honest pricing.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap restaurants near attractions
items.text: Restaurants directly facing the Great Wall, Forbidden City, or riverside promenades charge 3–5× normal prices for mediocre food. Walk 5–10 minutes away and prices drop dramatically with quality improving.

items.label: Hotel restaurants and malls
items.text: Safe but bland and expensive. Shopping malls (especially basement food courts) offer decent mid-range options at fairer prices than street level.

items.label: Use translation apps for characters
items.text: Google Translate's camera feature works instantly on menus—point your phone at the characters and get English. This is faster and more reliable than asking staff in many regions.

items.label: Recognise spice levels
items.text: Red chilli peppers (🌶️) marked on menus indicate heat level—more peppers mean more spice. Sichuan dishes with black peppercorns (málà) cause mouth-numbing tingling, not just heat.

items.label: Watch the portion sizes
items.text: Dishes served in China are often larger than expected and meant for sharing. Order 1–2 dishes per person plus rice or noodles, not one dish per person.

items.label: Check prices before ordering
items.text: For seafood or wild vegetables, always confirm the price upfront—some restaurants charge per 100g without clear signage, leading to expensive surprises. Ask 'duōshao qián yī fèn?' (how much per portion?).

items.label: Look for speciality marks
items.text: Dishes with ★ or 本店招牌 (běn diàn zhāopái—house specialty) are usually the chef's best work and worth ordering.

items.label: Mapo Tofu [麻婆豆腐]
items.text: Silky tofu in a fiery Sichuan chilli oil with minced pork and Sichuan peppercorns. The málà (mouth-numbing heat) is addictive and the dish costs around 15–25 RMB at local spots.

items.label: Peking Duck [北京烤鸭]
items.text: Whole roasted duck carved tableside with paper-thin skin and tender meat, eaten wrapped in crepes with hoisin and scallions. Order at Beijing's famous spots like Quanjude or cheaper local restaurants.

items.label: Xiaolongbao [小笼包]
items.text: Delicate steamed pork dumplings with a soupy, gelatinous filling made from pork aspic. These Shanghai specialities are best eaten immediately while hot, then dipped in vinegar with sliced ginger.

items.label: Chongqing Chicken [辣子鸡]
items.text: Diced chicken stir-fried with whole dried chillies, peanuts, and Sichuan peppercorns—numbing, spicy, and wildly flavourful. Not for chilli-averse eaters but unforgettable.

items.label: Hand-Pulled Noodles [拉面]
items.text: Lanzhou-style beef noodle soup (牛肉面) is the most common—silky noodles in aromatic broth topped with tender beef, radish, and coriander. A full meal costs 12–20 RMB.

items.label: Street-Food Crepes [煎饼]
items.text: Thin savoury crepes stuffed with egg, crispy fried wonton, scallion, and spicy bean sauce, folded and eaten hot. A breakfast staple costing 3–5 RMB and completely satisfying.

items.label: Use chopsticks correctly (or ask for a fork)
items.text: Never stick chopsticks upright in rice—this mimics funeral offerings. If you struggle, politely ask for a fork (叉子, chāzi); locals won't mind and servers appreciate the courtesy.

items.label: Arrive on time for group meals
items.text: Lateness is considered disrespectful, especially at formal dinners or with business associates. If invited, arrive 5–10 minutes early.

items.label: Let the host order first
items.text: In formal settings, the oldest or highest-ranking person orders. Don't order for yourself until invited to do so. At casual meals with friends, it's fine to order what you want.

items.label: Don't pour your own drink—pour for others
items.text: At meals with others, fill neighbours' glasses first, never your own. They'll reciprocate. This applies to beer, wine, and baijiu. Never refill your own cup without offering to others.

items.label: Rotate the lazy Susan (turntable)
items.text: If dishes are on a rotating turntable, turn it gently and only to reach what you need. Don't hog a section for yourself. Take smaller portions to leave food for others.

items.label: Expect toasts and say 'Gānbēi!' (cheers)
items.text: At dinner, hosts or seniors may propose toasts with baijiu—it's polite to stand, make eye contact, and clink glasses. Saying 'Gānbēi!' (literally 'dry cup') signals respect, though you don't have to drink it all.

items.label: Burping and slurping are acceptable
items.text: Loud eating noises (especially when eating noodles) show enjoyment and are perfectly normal. You don't need to suppress them.

items.label: Baijiu—China's firewater
items.text: A clear, powerful grain alcohol (40–60% ABV) that dominates dinner toasts. Brands like Moutai and Wuliangye are premium and expensive; cheaper versions (15–30 RMB) are perfectly drinkable. Drink it in small sips or as toasts, never casually.

items.label: Beer culture
items.text: Tsingtao and Snow are the most common brands, cheap (5–8 RMB for 500mL), and lightly flavoured. Beer is often served ice-cold and is the safest choice for casual drinking without formal toasting.

items.label: Tea instead of alcohol
items.text: It's completely acceptable to decline alcohol and drink tea, especially if you're unwell or driving. No one will pressure you, and offering tea as an alternative is always respected.

items.label: Huangjiu and rice wine
items.text: Shaoxing wine (黄酒), a sweet fermented rice wine, is used in cooking and as a warm drink. It's gentler than baijiu and common in eastern China, costing 20–50 RMB a bottle.

items.label: Avoid tap water—drink bottled or boiled
items.text: Tap water is not potable in most regions. Restaurants provide free hot water (白开水, báikāi shuǐ) or you can buy bottled water. Tea and hot drinks are safer choices.

items.label: Coffee is emerging, especially in cities
items.text: Major cities like Beijing, Shanghai, and Chengdu now have vibrant specialty coffee scenes with independent roasters and third-wave cafés. Expect 25–40 RMB for a cappuccino in trendy neighbourhoods.

items.label: Instant coffee and powdered mixes are common
items.text: Nescafé and local brands like Yuanyang are ubiquitous in convenience stores and small cafés, often paired with condensed milk packets. Quality varies, but they're cheap (3–8 RMB) and convenient.

items.label: Yuanyang—the local hybrid drink
items.text: A beloved mixture of strong black tea and sweetened condensed milk (Hong Kong style), served hot or iced. It's cheaper (5–10 RMB) and more authentic than coffee for many Chinese drinkers.

items.label: Chain cafés are everywhere but soulless
items.text: Luckin Coffee, Manner Coffee, and Starbucks dominate urban areas with cheap drinks (15–25 RMB for specialty options). They're reliable but lack local character—seek out independent spots instead.

items.label: Cafés are social hubs, not grab-and-go
items.text: In China, cafés are for sitting, working, or meeting friends for hours, not rushing through. Expect minimal pressure to leave, free WiFi, and sometimes surprisingly good food.

items.label: Use Dianping (Chinese Yelp) to find real restaurants
items.text: Dianping (大众点评) shows user photos, prices, and honest reviews. It's far more reliable than Google Maps in China, where many restaurants lack online presence or accurate listings.

items.label: Payment methods: WeChat Pay and Alipay are essential
items.text: Cash is increasingly rare; most restaurants require mobile payment via WeChat Pay (微信支付) or Alipay (支付宝). These apps are free to set up and can be linked to foreign cards or bank accounts.

items.label: Tipping is not expected
items.text: Unlike Western countries, tipping is neither expected nor customary in China. Rounding up or leaving small change is kind but unnecessary.

items.label: Check for hidden fees (service charge, table charge)
items.text: Some upscale restaurants add a 10–15% service charge automatically. Ask about this upfront to avoid surprises. Cheaper restaurants never charge this.

items.label: Eat where locals eat—follow the crowds
items.text: The busiest, most crowded restaurant with the longest queues is almost always the best choice. Local preferences don't lie.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around China.

items.label: Book via 12306 or apps
items.text: Use the official China Railways 12306 website or authorized apps like Trip.com and Fliggy to book tickets. English language support is available, though the official 12306 app works best with a Chinese phone number and real-name registration.

items.label: High-speed trains (G/D trains)
items.text: G-trains (高铁) are fastest and most comfortable, while D-trains (动车) are slightly slower but cheaper. Both require reserved seats and are ideal for routes between major cities like Beijing-Shanghai or Guangzhou-Shenzhen.

Ordinary trains (K/T/Z) are slowest and most crowded, especially during Chinese New Year and Golden Week holidays when tickets sell out weeks in advance. Avoid traveling during these peak periods if possible.

items.label: Real-name tickets required
items.text: All tickets require matching your ID passport number. Arrive 20-30 minutes early for high-speed trains and 60 minutes for ordinary trains.

items.label: Luggage and amenities
items.text: High-speed trains have luggage racks overhead and between cars. Bring your own food for long journeys, as dining car options are limited and overpriced.

items.label: Metro systems in major cities
items.text: Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Chengdu, and Wuhan have extensive metro networks that are clean, safe, and the fastest way to navigate cities. Download the local metro app (e.g., Beijing Metro app) or use Alipay/WeChat for QR code scanning at gates.

items.label: Unified payment systems
items.text: Most cities accept Alipay, WeChat Pay, and local transit cards. In smaller cities, purchase a rechargeable IC card (公交卡) at convenience stores or station booths for around 20 RMB.

items.label: Crowding during rush hours
items.text: Avoid metro travel during 7-9 AM and 5-7 PM on weekdays when trains are extremely crowded. Pickpocketing is rare but possible on packed trains.

items.label: Bus networks
items.text: Buses are cheap (1-3 RMB per ride) and cover areas metros don't reach, but routes are confusing without local knowledge. Use Amap or Baidu Maps apps to plan bus routes with real-time tracking.
Bus drivers may not speak English and stops aren't always announced clearly. Ask the driver to alert you when your stop arrives or use the maps app location feature.

sections.title: Taxis & Ride Apps [出租车网约车]

items.label: Ride-hailing apps preferred
items.text: Didi Chuxing dominates China's ride-sharing market with options like Didi Express (similar to Uber X) and Didi Premium. Download the app, add a Chinese phone number and WeChat/Alipay payment, then book rides with your destination address.

items.label: Traditional taxis
items.text: Street taxis are metered, reliable, and cheap (starting at 10-13 RMB), but hailing can be difficult in rush hours. Ensure the meter is running and drivers cannot refuse short trips legally.

items.label: Scams and overcharging
items.text: Some taxis in tourist areas or late at night may take longer routes or turn off meters. Ride-hailing apps are safer as pricing is fixed before booking and recorded for disputes.

items.label: Payment methods
items.text: Almost all taxis now accept mobile payment (Alipay/WeChat), though having small cash (50 RMB or less) is wise for older drivers. Didi automatically charges your registered payment method.

items.label: Airport and hotel bookings
items.text: Use Didi's premium 'airport express' option for guaranteed vehicles, or pre-book through your hotel concierge for peace of mind at major airports.

items.label: International driving permit limitations
items.text: Foreigners can legally drive in China with an International Driving Permit (IDP) and their home country license for up to 1 year, but enforcement varies and some rental companies refuse to rent to non-Chinese nationals. Check with your rental agency in advance.

items.label: Traffic and driving culture
items.text: Chinese traffic is chaotic with frequent red-light running, lane-changing without signals, and aggressive driving. Motorcycles and e-bikes weave unpredictably, and rules are enforced selectively by traffic police.

items.label: License plate restrictions
items.text: Major cities like Beijing and Shanghai use lottery systems or quotas to restrict new license plates; renting a car is much easier. Some cities ban out-of-province plates during rush hours (usually 7-10 AM and 4-7 PM).

items.label: Fuel and road conditions
items.text: Gas stations are common along highways but scarce in rural areas; plan accordingly. Expressways charge tolls (often 10-50 RMB per segment) via automatic payment or cash booths.

items.label: Use navigation apps
items.text: Baidu Maps and Amap show real-time traffic, speed cameras, and toll costs. Both apps work offline in limited capacity and are essential for navigation outside major cities.
Drunk driving fines start at 1,000 RMB and license suspension; drug driving results in criminal charges. Speeding fines range from 200-2,000 RMB depending on excess speed.

items.label: Major airports and airlines
items.text: Beijing Capital, Shanghai Pudong, and Guangzhou are China's largest hubs. China Eastern, China Southern, and Air China are the 'big three' carriers, while budget airlines like Spring Airlines and Southwest Air offer cheaper fares.

items.label: Check-in and security
items.text: Arrive 2-3 hours before international flights and 1.5 hours before domestic flights. Airport staff speak limited English outside major terminals; have your destination written in Chinese characters.

items.label: Book through platforms
items.text: Use Trip.com, Fliggy (Alibaba), or Ctrip for domestic flights; international flights are also available. Fares are often cheaper 2-4 weeks in advance, especially for domestic routes.

items.label: Delays and cancellations
items.text: Weather-related delays are common, especially in winter and during monsoon season. Airlines rarely offer compensation beyond rebooking, so travel insurance is recommended.

items.label: Airport transfers
items.text: Airport buses (机场大巴) are cheap and reliable, departing every 15-30 minutes. Alternatively, use Didi's airport express booking or pre-arrange hotel shuttle services.

items.label: River and coastal ferries
items.text: Ferries operate on major rivers like the Yangtze and Pearl River, and between islands (e.g., Hong Kong-Macau routes). Book through travel agents or official port websites; services are infrequent but scenic.

items.label: Tourist river cruises
items.text: Multi-day cruises along the Yangtze River between Chongqing and Wuhan are popular; book through operators like Yangtze River Cruises or travel agencies. Prices range from budget to luxury, typically 800-3,000 RMB per person.
Ferries are unreliable during typhoon season (June-September) and subject to sudden cancellations. Always check weather forecasts and have backup travel plans.

items.label: Documentation and safety
items.text: Passport and visa checks are standard on international ferries. Life jackets are provided but comfort and safety standards are lower than Western ferries.

items.label: Tourist passes and discounts
items.text: Some cities offer 1-3 day tourist metro passes (e.g., Shanghai, Chengdu), valid for unlimited travel. Student discounts (with valid student ID) are available on trains; age-based discounts vary by operator.

items.label: Group and advance booking
items.text: Booking 5+ train tickets together grants 5-10% discounts. Travel agencies often bundle transport with accommodation for better rates.

items.label: Ticket refunds and changes
items.text: Train refunds within 48 hours incur 5% penalties; after 48 hours, refunds are unavailable. Changes are allowed with minimal fees, but availability decreases near departure dates.

items.label: Age and ID requirements
items.text: Children under 1.2m travel free on trains and metro; 1.2-1.5m pay half-fare. Students require valid ID (国际学生证) for discounts; this card must be purchased in advance at your institution.

items.label: Digital ticketing
items.text: E-tickets via 12306, Trip.com, and airline apps eliminate paper tickets. Show QR codes on your phone at gates; ensure your phone is charged and connected to internet before travel.

items.label: Buy a local SIM card
items.text: Purchase a prepaid SIM at airports, convenience stores, or carrier shops (China Mobile, China Unicom, China Telecom) for 20-50 RMB with data packages. Activation requires your passport number; same-day activation is standard.

items.label: Tourist data packages
items.text: Carriers offer short-term packages: 5GB for 30 RMB (7-30 days) or 10GB for 50 RMB. Top-up data at convenience stores or via carrier apps (e.g., 中国移动 app for China Mobile).

items.label: Great Firewall restrictions
items.text: Google, Facebook, YouTube, and VPNs are blocked. Use Chinese apps instead: Baidu for search, WeChat for messaging, Alipay for payments. Download these before arriving if possible.

items.label: WiFi availability
items.text: Free WiFi is common at hotels, malls, and restaurants (usually requires phone number registration). Airport WiFi is reliable and requires passport verification.

items.label: Network coverage gaps
items.text: Remote rural areas and mountains may have no signal; download offline maps (Amap, Baidu Maps) before traveling. 4G coverage is excellent in cities but inconsistent on slow trains.

items.label: WeChat and Alipay are essential
items.text: These payment apps are required for taxis, shopping, and restaurants; most places no longer accept cash. Link your foreign bank card or credit card immediately upon arrival (via card or top-up services).
